rows.v: Old Fashioned, Sazerac, Mint Julep, Champagne Cocktail, Rum Old Fashioned, Oaxaca Old Fashioned
Spirit-forward and stirred — built around a base spirit, sugar, and bitters.

The word 'cocktail' first appeared in print in an 1806 New York newspaper, defined as spirits, sugar, water, and bitters — which is to say the Old Fashioned is the original cocktail, and every other drink is a variation on it.
Whiskey Sour, Daiquiri, Pisco Sour, Gimlet, Bee's Knees, Clover Club
The classic balance of spirit, citrus, and sweetener, shaken with ice.
Bartenders treat the Daiquiri as a litmus test — order one at a new bar, and how well they balance three simple ingredients (rum, lime, sugar) tells you whether to trust the rest of the menu.
Margarita, Sidecar, Cosmopolitan, Aviation, Last Word, Corpse Reviver No. 2
A sour built with a liqueur instead of plain sugar — often served over ice or up.

A Margarita and a Sidecar are the same drink in different clothes — both are 'daisies,' a sour where orange liqueur replaces the sugar; swap tequila-and-lime for brandy-and-lemon and you've crossed from one to the other.
Dry Martini, Manhattan, Martinez, Negroni, Boulevardier, Vieux Carré, Vesper
Spirit-forward and stirred, never shaken — clarity and chill are the point.
The Negroni was reputedly born when Count Camillo Negroni asked a Florence bartender to stiffen his Americano with gin in place of the soda — one swap, and a bitter icon was made.
Gin & Tonic, Tom Collins, Moscow Mule, Dark 'n' Stormy, Paloma, Mojito, Bloody Mary
Spirit plus a larger volume of mixer, served long over ice — easy-drinking and refreshing.
The gin and tonic exists because of malaria — British colonials took bitter quinine tonic as medicine and softened it with gin, lime, and sugar until the cure became the cocktail.
French 75, Aperol Spritz, Gin Fizz, Ramos Gin Fizz, Mimosa, Bellini
Topped with sparkling wine or soda for lift — best made just before serving.
The Ramos Gin Fizz is shaken so long — five minutes or more — that its New Orleans creator hired a relay line of 'shaker boys' to pass the tin down the bar.
Mai Tai, Piña Colada, Zombie, Jungle Bird, Hurricane
Multiple rums, fresh juices, and bold garnishes — built for warm weather and big flavor.
Tiki's two founding fathers, Don the Beachcomber and Trader Vic, both claimed to have invented the Mai Tai — and the recipe was guarded so jealously that the true spec was argued over for decades.
Espresso Martini, Irish Coffee, White Russian, Brandy Alexander
After-dinner sippers that double as dessert — rich, sweet, and often layered.
The Espresso Martini was invented in 1980s London when a customer asked Dick Bradsell for something to wake her up — and the coffee-and-vodka pick-me-up went on to define the after-dinner drink.
